chunk_summary: Ketogenic dietary therapies require supplementation and structured lab monitoring to prevent complications, with specific guidelines for hydration, micronutrients, and lab tests tailored to therapy duration and patient stability.
learning_objectives: Understand the essential supplements required for classic ratio-based ketogenic dietary therapy (KDT).
Learn the minimum lab monitoring schedule for patients on KDT, including frequency and key tests.
Recognize the importance of hydration and constipation prevention in KDT protocols.
Identify risk factors such as hypocitraturia and selenium deficiency in KDT patients.
key_facts: Minimum supplementation includes multivitamins with minerals (selenium), calcium plus vitamin D, and fiber for constipation prevention.
Early review within 1–2 weeks focuses on intake, hydration, and ketone/glucose monitoring techniques.
Lab tests include electrolytes, liver enzymes, fasting lipid profile, and urinalysis every 3 months in the first year, then every 6–12 months once stable.
Vitamin D status should be checked and repleted as needed; bone health surveillance is recommended for prolonged therapy.
In India, prioritize bicarbonate, lipids, and vitamin D monitoring if full panels are not feasible.
reference_quote: Selenium deficiency-induced cardiomyopathy during ketogenic diet therapy — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11575609/
document_key_topics: Minimum lab monitoring schedule for KDT
Hydration and constipation prevention in KDT
Vitamin D and bone health surveillance
